nativeRange.habitat: Inhabits coral and rubble areas of deeper lagoon and seaward reefs, typically reported at depths of about 10–50 m (recorded range 0–60 m); generally observed singly, grazing surface algal films and detritus from hard substrate

decorPreferences: live rock with biofilm and algal turf for grazing
rockwork crevices and overhangs for shelter
long horizontal footprint preferred over tall depth
sand bed for natural grazing behaviour

diet.notes: Uses individually movable bristle-like teeth to sift detritus and graze surface algal films from live rock. Marine-based seaweed (nori) and spirulina-rich herbivore preparations are generally advised at least three times per week. Has been reported to accept algae-based flakes/pellets, frozen vegetable preparations, and small meaty items (mysis, krill) in mixed-community feedings; vitamin-supplemented diets are commonly recommended to reduce risk of Head and Lateral Line Erosion.

goodWith: non-Ctenochaetus tangs (with caution and ample space)
avoidWith: similarly shaped or similarly coloured surgeonfish

imageCandidates.notes: Bernard Picton, 2000x1339. Tack-sharp lateral view showing the diagnostic orange-brown body, brilliant blue eye ring, and dark spot at the dorsal-fin base; whole fish in frame, well lit.
Diego Delso, 6790x4527. Ultra-high-res, tack-sharp reddish-brown individual with vivid blue-purple eye ring and fine spotting/lines; striking clean bokeh backdrop.
Bernard DUPONT from FRANCE, 2757x1654. Olive-brown individual with blue eye against vivid red soft coral; striking in-situ composition, whole fish in frame.

summary: The Two Spot Bristletooth Tang (Ctenochaetus binotatus) is an orange-brown Indo-Pacific surgeonfish with blue facial markings and two dark spots at the dorsal and anal-fin bases. Generally considered an efficient algae and detritus grazer, it is widely regarded as reef-safe and well suited to moderately sized reef systems.
careNotes:
The Two Spot Bristletooth Tang (Ctenochaetus binotatus) is a medium-sized Indo-Pacific surgeonfish named for the pair of dark spots at the base of its dorsal and anal fins. Adults are generally reported around 18–22 cm in length, and like all members of the bristletooth tang group, they are active grazers that spend most of the day working across live rock and rubble surfaces.

A tank of around 400 L (approximately 105 gallons) with a long horizontal footprint is generally advised. Bristletooth tangs graze throughout the day and benefit from plenty of open swimming space combined with extensive live rock that can develop the natural biofilm and algal turf they prefer to feed on. Rockwork crevices and overhangs provide important shelter, and moderate to strong water movement suits the species well. Water parameters should be stable and well-oxygenated within standard reef ranges (SG 1.022–1.026, pH 8.1–8.4, dKH 8–12).

Diet should lean heavily toward marine algae. Dried seaweed (nori) and spirulina-based herbivore foods at least three times per week are generally advised. The species has been reported to accept algae-based flakes and pellets, frozen vegetable preparations, and small meaty items such as mysis or krill during mixed-community feedings. Vitamin-rich diets are commonly recommended to reduce the risk of Head and Lateral Line Erosion (HLLE) — a condition in which small pits or channels develop along the head and lateral line; most acanthurids (surgeonfish) are reported to be susceptible, particularly when kept on a vitamin-deficient diet or in poor water quality. In the wild, the species has been documented grazing films of toxin-producing dinoflagellates (single-celled algae) from the substrate, which is the basis of historical ciguatera-poisoning reports associated with this species when consumed as food; this has no relevance to aquarium safety.

The Two Spot Bristletooth Tang is generally considered reef-safe. Reports of coral nipping are uncommon and tend to be associated with underfed individuals. The species is best kept singly — conspecific aggression toward other Ctenochaetus bristletooth tangs and similarly shaped surgeonfish has been widely reported — and is generally regarded as peaceable with non-tang reef fish such as clownfish, gobies, blennies, and wrasses.

As with all surgeonfish, the Two Spot Bristletooth Tang has sharp blade-like spines at the base of the tail (the caudal peduncle), sometimes called the 'scalpel'. These are used for defence and can inflict painful lacerations on handlers and on tankmates during disputes; care should be taken whenever the fish is handled or transferred.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding has not been documented for this species. Like most surgeonfish, the Two Spot Bristletooth Tang is presumed to be a pelagic broadcast spawner in the wild — meaning a mating pair releases eggs and sperm into open water, where the eggs develop as part of the plankton before eventually settling as juveniles. Achieving the conditions needed to trigger and sustain this process in a home aquarium is considered highly unlikely.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Species product page for Two Spot Bristletooth Tang. Provided max size (8 in / ~20 cm), tank minimum (70 gal / 265 L), temperature (72–78°F / 22–26°C), pH (8.1–8.4), SG (1.020–1.025), dKH (8–12), care level (moderate), diet (herbivore, seaweed 3x/week), temperament (semi-aggressive toward other tangs, peaceful otherwise), reef compatibility (yes), and family (Acanthuridae). Reached via the tangs-surgeons collection root supplied in the brief.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked species identity (Ctenochaetus binotatus Randall, 1955), family (Acanthuridae) and order (Acanthuriformes), max size (22.0 cm TL), depth range (0–60 m, typical 10–50 m), preferred temperature (24.5–28.9°C, mean 27.7°C), Indo-Pacific native range from East Africa to Tuamotu Islands (explicitly absent from Red Sea, Gulf of Oman, Hawaii), habitat (coral and rubble areas of deep lagoon and seaward reefs), IUCN status (Least Concern, 2010), and ecology (usually solitary, grazes surface algae and detritus, has been documented consuming toxin-producing dinoflagellates).

additional.name: Aquatics Unlimited
additional.notes: Hobby retailer secondary (Reef2Reef wiki had no species-specific page for C. binotatus). Confirmed max size (~8 in / 20 cm), minimum tank (90 gal / 340 L, larger than LiveAquaria's 70 gal), water parameters (72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12), semi-aggressive/territorial toward genus, reef-safe, and Indo-Pacific range. Stated 'to Hawaii' which conflicts with FishBase — Hawaii excluded from countries list.

additional.name: ReefApp Encyclopedia
additional.notes: Third hobby source consulted for tank-size triangulation. Stated max size 22 cm (8.7 in), minimum 500 L (~132 gal), temperature ~26°C, SG ~1.026, primarily algae/seaweed/spirulina diet, mostly peaceful but may show aggression toward similar species, always reef-safe.

fish.breedingNotes: No captive breeding records found in available hobby sources. Like most acanthurids, presumed pelagic broadcast spawner in the wild; spawning in aquaria is considered highly unlikely.
